legislation.title: Good Cause Eviction Law (Real Property Law Article 6-A)
legislation.summary: Enacted statewide rent control for market-rate apartments, with mandatory application in NYC. Caps rent increases at lower of 10% or CPI + 5%. Expires June 15, 2034.
legislation.forwardLooking: Law remains in effect through 2034 with annual CPI adjustments
legislation.whyItMatters: Does not apply to 70 Pine Street due to luxury rent exemption, but demonstrates regulatory trend toward expanded tenant protections

layers.name: Illinois Rent Control Preemption Act (50 ILCS 825)

rentControl.formula: No rent control permitted - landlords may raise rent without limitation

rentControl.investmentImplications: Provides maximum rent growth flexibility for landlords. No local jurisdiction can implement rent caps or stabilization measures without state legislative action to repeal the preemption act.

legislation.title: Tenant Protection Act (HB 3874)
legislation.status: Introduced 2021, still pending in legislature as of 2026
legislation.summary: Would overturn the Rent Control Preemption Act and allow local governments to enact rent control policies. Includes provisions for 5% annual rent increase cap, 180-day notice for larger increases, and prohibition of rent increases in first 12 months of tenancy.
legislation.forwardLooking: If passed, would fundamentally change Illinois rent regulation by allowing local rent control ordinances for the first time since 1997
legislation.whyItMatters: Could transform the regulatory landscape by permitting Chicago and other municipalities to implement rent stabilization measures

legislation.title: Initiative Petition to Protect Tenants by Limiting Rent Increases (2026 Ballot)
legislation.status: Certified - 88,132 valid signatures collected, pending legislative review
legislation.summary: Would replace Chapter 40P with statewide rent stabilization capping annual increases at CPI or 5% (whichever lower) with no vacancy decontrol. January 31, 2026 rent becomes permanent base. Exempts owner-occupied buildings ≤4 units and new construction for first 10 years.
legislation.forwardLooking: Strong polling support (56-63%) indicates high passage probability. Legislature has until first Wednesday in May 2026 to act before automatic ballot placement.
legislation.whyItMatters: Would fundamentally transform Massachusetts rental market by establishing strictest statewide rent control in U.S., eliminating vacancy decontrol entirely and creating permanent rent caps for existing buildings like 929 Mass Ave.

rentControl.summary: Temporary rent increase limitation for tenants over 62 whose primary income is Social Security or disability benefits, expires December 31, 2026
rentControl.applicability: Only applies to tenants 62+ with qualifying income sources in this specific property
rentControl.investmentImplications: Minimal impact - affects only qualifying senior tenants and expires in 2026. Does not create permanent rent control framework or precedent for broader regulations.

sources.title: 2024 Legislative Updates - Georgia Apartment Association
sources.note: HB 938 details and industry analysis

governingStructure.overlappingRegime: Single-layer state prohibition with limited senior protection overlay
governingStructure.structuralInterpretation: Clear hierarchical structure with state law supremacy over local ordinances
governingStructure.applicabilityRiskNote: Extremely low regulatory risk due to comprehensive state preemption and politically unfavorable environment for rent control

finalApplicability.primaryGoverningRule: Georgia O.C.G.A. § 44-7-19 prohibits all local rent control
finalApplicability.overlayRules: HB 938 temporary senior protection through 2026; HB 404 habitability requirements
finalApplicability.reasoning: Georgia statute enacted in 1984 completely prohibits counties and municipalities from regulating private residential rent amounts. Property built in 2002 has no rent control protections regardless of age.
finalApplicability.ageInterpretation: Building age irrelevant - state preemption applies universally to all private rentals
riskFlags: Monitor HB 938 renewal beyond 2026 for senior protections
Track habitability compliance under HB 404 Safe at Home Act
finalApplicability.underwritingRentGrowthFormula: Market rate with 60-day notice requirement (no percentage cap except 10% for qualifying seniors through 2026)
finalApplicability.investmentImplications: Excellent landlord-favorable regulatory environment with unlimited rent growth potential. State preemption provides strong protection against future local rent control initiatives. Minimal compliance burden beyond standard landlord-tenant obligations and new habitability standards. Political environment strongly favors property rights with failed rent control repeal attempts in 2023-2024 legislative sessions.

legislation.title: O.C.G.A. § 44-7-19 - Rent Control Prohibition
legislation.summary: Prohibits all counties and municipalities from enacting ordinances regulating rent amounts for privately owned residential properties
legislation.forwardLooking: Stable - no credible repeal efforts in Republican-controlled legislature
legislation.whyItMatters: Provides comprehensive protection against local rent control initiatives, ensuring unlimited rent increase flexibility subject only to notice requirements

legislation.title: HB 404 - Safe at Home Act
legislation.summary: Establishes habitability warranty, 3-day eviction grace period, and caps security deposits at 2 months rent
legislation.forwardLooking: Compliance required - affects property maintenance obligations but not rent control
legislation.whyItMatters: Creates new landlord maintenance obligations and procedural requirements but does not restrict rent increases

regulatoryOutcome.naReason: State preemption under O.C.G.A. § 44-7-19 with no active repeal legislation or pending regulatory changes. Failed rent control repeal attempts (HB 852) in 2024 legislative session demonstrate political unfeasibility in current Republican-controlled environment.

layers.name: Washington HB 1217 Rent Stabilization
layers.basis: Statewide rent control law effective May 7, 2025

layers.name: King County
layers.basis: Extended notice requirements for rent increases

rentControl.formula: 7% + CPI, capped at 10% annually (when applicable after exemption expires)
rentControl.summary: Washington HB 1217 provides 12-year exemption for new construction from first certificate of occupancy. This building qualifies for exemption until approximately 2030-2031.
rentControl.applicability: Building is exempt from state rent control until 12 years after certificate of occupancy (2030-2031). After exemption expires, rent increases will be capped at 7% plus Consumer Price Index or 10%, whichever is less.
rentControl.investmentImplications: Significant regulatory advantage during exemption period allows market-rate rent optimization. However, investors must plan for meaningful constraint on rent growth beginning in 2030-2031. 90-day advance notice will be required for all increases once exemption expires.
rentControl.certainty: High - exemption clearly established in statute

sources.title: HB 1217 Landlord Resource Center - WA Department of Commerce
sources.note: Official state guidance on rent control implementation

rentControl.formula: No rent cap; extended notice periods for increases >3%
rentControl.summary: King County requires 120-day notice for rent increases exceeding 3% in unincorporated areas. Seattle is incorporated and follows city ordinances instead.
rentControl.applicability: Property is located within Seattle city limits, so King County unincorporated area rules do not apply. Seattle city ordinances govern instead.
rentControl.investmentImplications: King County rules do not directly apply to this Seattle property, but demonstrate regional trend toward tenant protections and extended notice requirements.
rentControl.certainty: High - property is within Seattle city limits

sources.title: Rent Increase Ordinances by City - WA Landlord Association
sources.note: Summary of local rent increase notice requirements

rentControl.ruleDateBasis: Seattle Municipal Code rent increase ordinances
rentControl.formula: No rent cap; 180-day advance notice required; EDRA triggered at 10%+ increases
rentControl.summary: Seattle requires 180-day advance written notice for all rent increases. Increases of 10% or more within 12 months trigger Economic Displacement Relocation Assistance (EDRA) requirements.
rentControl.applicability: All rental properties in Seattle must provide 180-day advance notice for rent increases. Properties must attach EDRA notice for increases ≥10% and may owe relocation assistance to qualifying tenants.
rentControl.investmentImplications: Long notice periods require advance planning for rent optimization strategies. EDRA requirements create potential relocation costs for large increases, encouraging smaller, more frequent adjustments. Notice must include specific Seattle-required language and cannot be enforced if property fails to meet RRIO standards.

governingStructure.localRegulationAuthority: Washington allows local tenant protections while state law provides baseline rent stabilization framework
governingStructure.localRuleStrength: Strong - Seattle has comprehensive tenant protections including extended notice requirements and relocation assistance
governingStructure.overlappingRegime: State rent control with local notice and tenant protection overlays
governingStructure.primaryRule: Washington HB 1217 (12-year new construction exemption applies)
governingStructure.overlayRules: Seattle 180-day notice requirement and EDRA relocation assistance
governingStructure.structuralInterpretation: State provides rent cap framework with local jurisdictions maintaining authority over notice periods and tenant protections
governingStructure.applicabilityRiskNote: Complex interaction between state exemption period and local notice requirements creates regulatory transition risk in 2030-2031

finalApplicability.overlayRules: Seattle 180-day advance notice requirement and EDRA relocation assistance
finalApplicability.reasoning: Building completed in 2018-2019 qualifies for 12-year exemption from state rent control until approximately 2030-2031. During exemption period, no state-imposed rent caps apply, but Seattle local ordinances still require 180-day advance notice and EDRA compliance for increases ≥10%.
finalApplicability.ageInterpretation: 8 years old as of 2026, with 4-5 years remaining in new construction exemption period
riskFlags: State rent control will begin applying in 2030-2031 when exemption expires
Seattle algorithmic pricing ordinance became effective July 31, 2025
EDRA relocation assistance requirements for increases ≥10%
finalApplicability.underwritingRentGrowthFormula: Market rate (no cap) until 2030-2031, then min(7% + CPI, 10%) annually
finalApplicability.investmentImplications: Property currently enjoys optimal regulatory environment for rent optimization with no state-imposed caps during new construction exemption period. However, underwriting must account for meaningful constraint beginning in 2030-2031 when state rent control applies. Seattle's 180-day notice requirement enables strategic rent planning but requires advance coordination. EDRA requirements suggest smaller, more frequent increases may be preferable to large annual adjustments to avoid relocation assistance obligations.

legislation.title: House Bill 1217 - Rent Stabilization
legislation.summary: Statewide rent control limiting annual increases to 7% plus CPI (max 10%). Includes 12-year new construction exemption, 90-day notice requirements, and 15-year sunset clause. Manufactured homes capped at 5%.
legislation.forwardLooking: Law is in effect with exemption period for this property expiring in 2030-2031
legislation.whyItMatters: First statewide rent control in Washington, fundamentally changing investment landscape. New construction exemption provides temporary regulatory advantage but creates future constraint for newer properties.

sources.title: Washington Governor Signs Rent Control Into Law - Multifamily Dive
sources.note: Industry analysis of HB 1217 passage and implications

legislation.title: Council Bill 121000 - Algorithmic Pricing Ordinance
legislation.summary: Prohibits use of algorithmic pricing software that coordinates rent recommendations using data from multiple properties. Violations subject to $7,500 penalties plus private right of action.
legislation.forwardLooking: Currently in effect with enforcement actions already taken
legislation.whyItMatters: Restricts use of revenue management software platforms, potentially impacting rent optimization strategies for larger operators.
